title: Chameleon (genome): Polymorphic UDP Tunneling Protocol
description:
Chameleon is a polymorphic UDP tunneling protocol implemented in Go. Each session deterministically derives a unique wire format (“genome”) from a pre-shared key (PSK) via HKDF-SHA256. The resulting traffic appears as pure noise with approximately 7.9 bits/byte Shannon entropy — no magic bytes, fixed headers, or recognizable handshake patterns exist on the wire.

Applications connect through a local SOCKS5 proxy (RFC 1928/1929). Multiple TCP streams are multiplexed over a single encrypted UDP tunnel using a custom reliable transport with selective acknowledgment (SACK), NewReno congestion control, and per-stream flow control.

Key properties:

  • Session-unique wire format: field order (Fisher-Yates shuffle), 0–3 decoy fields, variable nonce size (12/24 bytes), four length encoding schemes, randomized padding
  • AEAD encryption: ChaCha20-Poly1305, XChaCha20-Poly1305, or AES-256-GCM
  • Anti-replay protection: sliding window with random initial epoch
  • Reliable delivery over UDP: per-stream sequence numbers, SACK, fast retransmit, RTT estimation (Jacobson/Karels)
  • Zero recognizable signatures for deep packet inspection (DPI)
notes: Chameleon derives a per-session wire format ('genome') from a PSK using HKDF, making each session structurally unique. This approach to protocol polymorphism may be of interest to network security researchers studying DPI evasion and traffic analysis resistance.
references: RFC 1929 - Username/Password Authentication for SOCKS V5
RFC 5869 - HMAC-based Extract-and-Expand Key Derivation Function (HKDF)
RFC 8439 - ChaCha20 and Poly1305 for IETF Protocols
